questions.question: Why are more metrics not automatically better?
options: Because large metric sets can add cost and noise without narrowing the debugging space.
Because metric platforms can only store exactly ten metrics per service.
Because metrics are only useful for batch systems, not APIs.
Because traces make all metrics unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Useful metrics reduce ambiguity; extra low-signal metrics often do the opposite.

questions.question: What kind of metric is often most useful for an async pipeline?
options: Queue age or end-to-end lag, because it tracks delayed user impact directly.
Source file count in the worker repository.
The number of runbooks linked from the dashboard.
The total memory in the on-call engineer's laptop.
questions.explanation: Queue age often reflects whether work is becoming stale or delayed for users.

questions.question: Which metric best helps explain a latency incident with no obvious local CPU saturation?
options: Dependency latency and error rate broken out by downstream service.
The number of markdown files in the repo.
The median age of closed pull requests.
A single daily average of total request count.
questions.explanation: If local saturation is absent, downstream behavior is a strong next hypothesis.

questions.question: Why are latency histograms or percentiles usually better than averages alone?
options: They reveal tail pain that averages can hide even when users are timing out.
They eliminate all cost from metrics storage.
They guarantee every slow request will be traced automatically.
They are only needed when traffic is below 1 QPS.
questions.explanation: Tail latency often matters more operationally than average latency.

questions.question: What is the main risk of putting raw `tenant_id` on default high-volume request metrics?
options: It can create massive cardinality and make the metrics backend expensive or unstable.
It automatically disables region filtering.
It prevents alerts from being created at all.
It makes logs impossible to correlate.
questions.explanation: Unbounded labels can explode the number of stored time series.

questions.question: Which combination is strongest for a synchronous API service?
options: User success and latency, dependency health, and at least one saturation signal.
CPU percent alone plus a weekly dashboard screenshot.
Only request count, because success can be inferred later.
Only trace sampling rate, because metrics are redundant.
questions.explanation: A balanced set connects user outcome, bottlenecks, and capacity pressure.

questions.question: What hidden trade-off appears when adding many detailed dimensions to metrics?
options: You gain drill-down power, but storage cost and query latency can rise sharply.
You lose the ability to collect request counts entirely.
You guarantee that dashboards will stay simple to read.
You remove the need for logs or traces.
questions.explanation: Richer labels can help diagnosis, but they are never free.

questions.question: What is a strong answer to 'Which metric would you inspect first during a backlog incident?'
options: A metric tied to work delay such as queue age or end-to-end lag, because it best reflects whether the system is keeping up.
The number of developers in the owning team.
The age of the oldest README file in the repository.
A chart of hostnames sorted alphabetically.
questions.explanation: Delay-oriented metrics usually reveal backlog harm faster than raw activity counters.
